text: The patient is positioned in a modified lithotomy position, and optical entry is achieved in the suprambulical area using a 5mm port. Significant pelvic adhesions are visible upon entry into the abdominal cavity. Right-sided ports are used for adhesiolysis, revealing the infrared-lit right ureter, while the left ureter remains obscured by adhesions. The sigmoid colon is identified with a normal mesentery and a large inflammatory process in the pelvis.

The surgeon uses traction and counter-traction to define and separate the plane between the sigmoid colon and the pelvic sidewall, carefully avoiding injury to retroperitoneal structures. Dissection continues between the right and left sides of the sigmoid colon, revealing the plane between the colon and the bladder. The bladder is filled with normal saline, demonstrating its adherence to the intra-abdominal wall. The distal sigmoid colon appears relatively normal, but remains densely adhered to the bladder, with the left ureter involved in the inflammatory process.

The surgeon considers options for dividing the proximal sigmoid colon to improve visualization and mobilization. A wide-jawed grasper and finger fracture technique are suggested to define the plane between the bladder and sigmoid colon. The paring vocal port is replaced with a hand port to aid in retraction and dissection. The issue with the left ureter not lighting up is demonstrated as the optical fiber was inserted too far, corrected by pulling back the fiber until the reference mark is visible, shown by turning off the laparoscope light source.

The surgeon uses intra-abdominal hand retraction to free the displaced ureter from its attachments to the inflammatory process. The colovesical fistula is carefully dissected, revealing a small fistula tract between the colon and bladder amidst a large area of inflammatory adhesions. The adhesions are gently taken down, and the dissection proceeds from the abdomen towards the pelvis and vice versa until only the small fistula tract remains, which is transected using harmonic shears.

The surgeon selects the distal resection point on the normal-appearing distal sigmoid colon and rectum, firing the stapler across this location. With the sigmoid colon freed from the pelvis, pelvic structures are visualized, including both ureters and the bladder. Methylene blue dye is injected into the bladder via Foley catheter, revealing a small hint of blue at the fistula site without urine extravasation. A colorectal anastomosis is performed using a 29mm circular stapler, followed by the creation of a J-type omental flap, which is secured in the pelvis to protect the anastomosis and fistula site.
